era_context: The Roman Empire is in the early stages of a civil war between Constantius II and the usurper Magnentius, which began in 350 CE. Magnentius controls much of the Western Empire (Gaul, Spain, Britain, North Africa), while Constantius II controls the East. The conflict is beginning to drain resources and military manpower from both sides. In the East, the Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II remains a persistent threat. Christianity is consolidating its position as the dominant religion, with theological disputes over Arianism shaping imperial policy.

events.title: Magnentius consolidates control of Western Roman Empire
events.region: Roman Empire (Gaul, Spain, Britain, North Africa)
events.description: Following his usurpation in 350 CE, Magnentius consolidates his control over the Western Roman Empire. He controls Gaul, Spain, Britain, and North Africa, and is recognized as emperor by the armies and populations of these regions. Magnentius attempts to legitimize his rule by minting coins, issuing edicts, and maintaining the administrative structures of the empire. However, his rule is contested by Constantius II, who controls the Eastern Empire and is preparing military campaigns to reclaim the West. The year 351 CE marks the height of Magnentius's power, before Constantius II's military campaigns begin to turn the tide against him.
events.certainty_note: Magnentius's consolidation of power in the Western Empire is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 351 CE is widely accepted as marking the period of his greatest territorial control.

events.title: Constantius II begins military campaigns against Magnentius
events.description: Constantius II, having consolidated his control of the Eastern Empire, begins military campaigns against the usurper Magnentius. The campaigns start in the Balkans and along the Danube frontier, with Constantius II's forces gradually pushing westward. The civil war, which will last until 353 CE, is now entering its active military phase. Constantius II's superior resources and military organization give him an advantage, but the campaigns are costly and will require several years to complete. The year 351 CE marks the beginning of the military phase of the civil war.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Constantius II's military campaigns beginning in 351 CE is well-attested, but specific battles and dates are not always clearly documented. The year is part of the broader civil war (350–353 CE).
cross_references: Part of broader civil war (350–353 CE)

events.title: Shapur II of Sassanid Persia campaigns in Mesopotamia
events.description: Shapur II, the Sassanid Persian king, continues his military campaigns in Mesopotamia and the Caucasus region. These campaigns are part of a broader pattern of Sassanid expansion and consolidation of control over territories contested with the Roman Empire. Shapur II is one of the most militarily active Sassanid rulers, and his campaigns pose a significant threat to Roman interests in the East. The exact details of campaigns in 351 CE are sparse, but Shapur II's reign (309–379 CE) is marked by continuous military activity. The campaigns are particularly significant because they occur while Constantius II is distracted by the civil war with Magnentius in the West.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (later Islamic source)

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's reign is well-documented, but specific campaigns in 351 CE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader pattern of Sassanid military activity during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman-Sassanid conflict (3rd–7th centuries CE)
Contemporary with Constantius II's civil war with Magnentius

events.title: Synod of Sirmium (first) on Arian controversy
events.description: A church synod is held in Sirmium to address the ongoing Arian controversy. The synod produces a creed that attempts to find a middle ground between Nicene orthodoxy and Arianism, but which is generally seen as leaning toward Arianism. Constantius II, who favors a semi-Arian position, uses imperial authority to enforce the synod's decisions. The synod represents an early stage in the series of councils and synods that will dominate the 350s CE, as Constantius II attempts to impose theological uniformity on the empire. The synod's creed is controversial and will be rejected by later councils.
events.certainty_note: The Synod of Sirmium is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is debated. Some sources place it in 351 CE, others in 352 CE or later. The synod's canons are known, but the precise attendees and proceedings are reconstructed from fragmentary sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Arian controversy (325–381 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Synod of Sirmium is disputed among modern scholars. Some sources place the first synod in 351 CE, others in 352 CE, and still others in later years. The exact number of synods held in Sirmium is also debated—some scholars identify three or four separate synods, while others see them as a single ongoing process. The synod's exact significance and the nature of its creed are also debated. Additionally, the extent to which Constantius II personally endorsed the synod's decisions is unclear from the surviving sources. Regarding Shapur II's campaigns, the specific military actions in 351 CE are not clearly documented in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 351 CE is moderately rich for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding political and military events. Ammianus Marcellinus is the most detailed source, but he is writing retrospectively and may have biases. Ecclesiastical sources provide detailed information about the Arian controversy but are less concerned with political and military events. The record for the Sassanid Empire is limited and often filtered through later Islamic historiography. The narrative is heavily shaped by court-sponsored histories and later Christian compilations, which may emphasize theological disputes over political and military realities. The record for regions outside the Mediterranean and Near East is virtually absent.

graph_edges.note: Magnentius's consolidation of power in the West prompts Constantius II to begin military campaigns against him
Constantius II's initial campaigns in 351 lead to continued campaigns in Gaul in 352
The first Synod of Sirmium in 351 leads to further Arian synods, including the second Synod of Sirmium in 352
